<!-- Topic 2: Core Loop Patterns -->
<!-- Total slides: 4 -->

# Core Loop Patterns

---

## You know three loop types — but what are loops actually for? {.smaller}

Knowing the syntax of `while`, `for`, and `do-while` is not the same as knowing when and why to use them. Loop patterns are the recurring solutions that show up across every program you will write.

::: notes
Total slides: 4
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Three Pattern Categories

Loops fall into three conceptual groups based on what they do:

- **Control Patterns** — decide when to stop: sentinel loops, flag-controlled loops, input validation loops
- **State Tracking Patterns** — accumulate information across iterations: counters, running totals, min/max tracking
- **Structural Patterns** — move through a collection: iteration over arrays and vectors

Each category has a different purpose. The syntax follows from the purpose.

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## What Loops Are Used For

Every loop you write does one of these four things:

1. Repeat an action a controlled number of times
2. Process every element in a collection
3. Track or accumulate information over time
4. Keep running until a condition occurs

When you are stuck on a loop problem, identify which of these applies first.

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Summary

Loops are not just syntax — they are patterns. Classifying a loop by what it does (control, state tracking, structural) tells you which variables to initialize, what the condition should test, and what belongs in the body.

<!-- Slide 4 -->
